notes.title: The Living World — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Characteristics of life, taxonomy and the classification hierarchy.

## 1. Characteristics of Living Organisms
- **Growth** (twin: increase in mass & number), **reproduction**, **metabolism** (defining feature), **cellular organisation**, **consciousness** (most obvious defining property), self-replication, response to stimuli.
- Metabolism + cellular organisation are defining; growth & reproduction are NOT (mules, sterile organisms reproduce-less yet living).

## 2. Taxonomy & Systematics
- **Taxonomy:** identification, nomenclature, classification.
- **Binomial nomenclature** (Linnaeus): Genus + species, italics, genus capitalised. e.g. Homo sapiens.

## 3. Taxonomic Hierarchy
- Kingdom → Phylum/Division → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species.
- Species is the basic unit; higher categories show fewer common characters.

## 4. Taxonomic Aids
- Herbarium, botanical gardens, museum, zoological parks, key, flora, manuals, monographs.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Metabolism and cellular organisation are DEFINING; growth/reproduction are not exclusive to living things.
- In binomial names, genus is capitalised, species is not; both italicised.
- Going up the hierarchy, the number of common characteristics decreases.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Defining: metabolism, cellular organisation, consciousness.
- Binomial nomenclature rules.
- Hierarchy: King Philip Came Over For Good Soup.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the order of the taxonomic hierarchy and one example name — direct one-mark questions every year.

notes.title: Biological Classification — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Whittaker's five-kingdom system and the major groups including viruses.

## 1. Five Kingdom Classification (Whittaker)
- **Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia** — based on cell structure, body organisation, nutrition, reproduction.

## 2. Monera (Bacteria)
- Prokaryotic. Shapes: coccus, bacillus, vibrio, spirillum.
- Archaebacteria (extreme habitats), Eubacteria. Cyanobacteria (photosynthetic). Nutrition: autotrophic/heterotrophic.

## 3. Protista
- Eukaryotic, mostly unicellular. Chrysophytes (diatoms), dinoflagellates, euglenoids, slime moulds, protozoans (amoeboid, flagellated, ciliated, sporozoans).

## 4. Fungi
- Heterotrophic (saprophytic/parasitic), cell wall of chitin. Classes: Phycomycetes, Ascomycetes, Basidiomycetes, Deuteromycetes.
- Symbiotic associations: lichens (fungi + algae), mycorrhiza (fungi + roots).

## 5. Viruses, Viroids, Lichens
- Viruses: non-cellular, obligate parasites (DNA or RNA). TMV (RNA), bacteriophage (DNA).
- Viroids (free RNA), prions (protein).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Viruses are NOT in the five-kingdom system (non-cellular).
- Fungal cell wall = chitin; plant = cellulose.
- Diatoms have silica cell walls ('diatomaceous earth').

## 📝 Quick Recap
- 5 kingdoms: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia.
- Fungi classes & symbioses.
- Viruses: DNA or RNA, obligate parasites.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember the basis of classification and one example per group — NEET asks matching-type questions here.

notes.title: Cell: The Unit of Life — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Prokaryotic vs eukaryotic cells and the structure/function of organelles.

## 1. Cell Theory
- All organisms made of cells; cell is the basic structural & functional unit; new cells arise from pre-existing cells (Virchow).

## 2. Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic
- Prokaryote: no nucleus/membrane organelles, 70S ribosomes, single circular DNA.
- Eukaryote: true nucleus, membrane-bound organelles, 80S ribosomes (70S in mitochondria/chloroplast).

## 3. Organelles
- **Mitochondria** (power house, ATP, double membrane, cristae).
- **Chloroplast** (photosynthesis, thylakoids/grana).
- **ER** (rough — protein, smooth — lipid), **Golgi** (packaging/secretion), **lysosomes** (suicidal bags), **ribosomes** (protein synthesis), **vacuoles**.
- **Nucleus:** nuclear envelope, chromatin, nucleolus (rRNA).

## 4. Cell Membrane & Wall
- Fluid mosaic model (Singer & Nicolson). Cell wall (cellulose) in plants.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Mitochondria & chloroplast have their OWN DNA and 70S ribosomes (semi-autonomous).
- Ribosomes are non-membranous; lysosomes are single-membranous.
- Plant cells have cell wall + chloroplast + large vacuole; animal cells have centrioles.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Cell theory; pro vs eukaryote (70S vs 80S).
- Mitochondria/chloroplast semi-autonomous.
- Fluid mosaic membrane.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the distinguishing features of prokaryotic vs eukaryotic cells — a guaranteed comparison question.

notes.title: Biomolecules (Biology) — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Chemical constituents of living cells: carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, nucleic acids and enzymes.

## 1. Biomacromolecules
- Primary metabolites (amino acids, sugars) vs secondary metabolites (alkaloids, pigments).
- Macromolecules: proteins, polysaccharides, nucleic acids (>1000 Da).

## 2. Proteins & Amino Acids
- 20 amino acids; peptide bonds. Structure levels: primary → secondary → tertiary → quaternary.
- Collagen (most abundant animal protein), RuBisCO (most abundant enzyme/protein in biosphere).

## 3. Carbohydrates & Lipids
- Monosaccharides, polysaccharides (starch, cellulose, glycogen, chitin).
- Lipids: fats, oils, phospholipids (membrane).

## 4. Enzymes
- Biocatalysts; lower activation energy. **Mechanism:** enzyme–substrate complex, active site.
- Factors: temperature, pH (optimum), substrate concentration.
- Cofactors: prosthetic groups, coenzymes, metal ions. Competitive inhibition (e.g. malonate vs succinate).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- RuBisCO is the most abundant protein/enzyme.
- Enzymes lower activation energy but do not change equilibrium.
- Competitive inhibitors resemble the substrate and bind the active site.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Macromolecules & metabolites.
- Protein structure levels.
- Enzymes: active site, cofactors, inhibition.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Understand competitive vs non-competitive inhibition with the malonate–succinate example.

notes.title: Cell Cycle and Cell Division — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
The cell cycle, mitosis and meiosis with their significance.

## 1. Cell Cycle Phases
- **Interphase** (G₁, S, G₂): S phase = DNA replication. G₀ = quiescent stage.
- **M phase:** mitosis (karyokinesis) + cytokinesis.

## 2. Mitosis (equational)
- Stages: Prophase, Metaphase (chromosomes at equator), Anaphase (sister chromatids separate), Telophase.
- Produces 2 identical diploid daughter cells. Occurs in somatic cells (growth, repair).

## 3. Meiosis (reductional)
- Two divisions (Meiosis I & II); produces 4 haploid cells.
- **Meiosis I:** homologous pairing, **crossing over** (Pachytene) → genetic variation; reduction of chromosome number.
- **Meiosis II:** like mitosis.

## 4. Significance
- Mitosis: growth & repair. Meiosis: gamete formation, maintains chromosome number, generates variation.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- DNA replicates in S phase (not M phase).
- Crossing over occurs in Pachytene of Prophase I.
- Sister chromatids separate in mitotic anaphase and anaphase II (not anaphase I).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Interphase (G₁, S, G₂) + M phase.
- Mitosis → 2 diploid; Meiosis → 4 haploid.
- Crossing over = variation.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn exactly what separates in each anaphase (homologues in I, chromatids in II/mitosis).

notes.title: Photosynthesis in Higher Plants — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
How green plants convert light energy into chemical energy.

## 1. Site & Pigments
- Occurs in chloroplasts. Pigments: chlorophyll a (primary), chlorophyll b, carotenoids (accessory).
- Chlorophyll a is the reaction centre.

## 2. Light Reaction (in thylakoids)
- Photosystems PS I (P700) and PS II (P680).
- **Z-scheme:** electron flow PS II → PS I → NADP⁺.
- Splitting of water (photolysis) releases O₂. Produces ATP (photophosphorylation) and NADPH.
- Cyclic (only PS I, only ATP) vs non-cyclic (both PS, ATP + NADPH + O₂).

## 3. Dark Reaction (Calvin cycle, in stroma)
- **C₃ cycle:** CO₂ fixed by RuBisCO onto RuBP → 3-PGA. Uses ATP & NADPH.
- **C₄ plants** (Hatch–Slack): first product OAA (4-carbon); Kranz anatomy; more efficient, no photorespiration.

## 4. Photorespiration & Limiting Factors
- Photorespiration (in C₃) wastes energy (RuBisCO acts as oxygenase).
- **Blackman's law of limiting factors:** rate limited by the factor in shortest supply (light, CO₂, temperature).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- O₂ in photosynthesis comes from WATER (photolysis), not CO₂.
- C₄ plants lack photorespiration; more efficient in hot climates.
- Light reaction in thylakoid; Calvin cycle in stroma.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- PS II (P680), PS I (P700); Z-scheme.
- C₃ (3-PGA) vs C₄ (OAA, Kranz).
- O₂ from water.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember C₄ advantages (no photorespiration, Kranz anatomy) — frequently compared with C₃ in NEET.

notes.title: Respiration in Plants — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Cellular respiration: glycolysis, Krebs cycle, ETC and energy yield.

## 1. Glycolysis (cytoplasm)
- Glucose → 2 pyruvate; net gain 2 ATP + 2 NADH. Common to aerobic & anaerobic.

## 2. Fermentation (anaerobic)
- Pyruvate → ethanol + CO₂ (yeast) or lactic acid (muscle). Little ATP.

## 3. Aerobic Respiration (mitochondria)
- **Link reaction:** pyruvate → acetyl-CoA.
- **Krebs cycle (TCA):** in matrix; produces NADH, FADH₂, ATP, CO₂.
- **ETC & oxidative phosphorylation:** on inner membrane; O₂ is final electron acceptor; ATP synthase makes ATP.

## 4. Energy Yield & RQ
- ~36–38 ATP per glucose (aerobic).
- **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** = CO₂ released/O₂ consumed: carbohydrates = 1, fats <1, proteins ~0.9.

## 5. Amphibolic Pathway
- Respiration is amphibolic (both catabolic and anabolic — intermediates used for synthesis).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Glycolysis occurs in cytoplasm; Krebs in mitochondrial matrix; ETC on inner membrane.
- O₂ is the FINAL electron acceptor (not in glycolysis).
- RQ of fats is less than 1.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Glycolysis (2 ATP) → Krebs → ETC (~36–38 ATP).
- Fermentation: ethanol/lactic acid.
- RQ: carbs 1, fats <1.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Know the location of each stage and the RQ values — high-frequency NEET facts.

notes.title: Breathing and Exchange of Gases — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Human respiratory system, mechanism of breathing and gas transport.

## 1. Respiratory System
- Nostrils → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli (site of gas exchange).
- Diaphragm and intercostal muscles drive breathing.

## 2. Mechanism of Breathing
- **Inspiration:** diaphragm contracts (flattens), external intercostals contract → thoracic volume up, pressure down → air in.
- **Expiration:** muscles relax → volume down → air out.

## 3. Respiratory Volumes & Capacities
- Tidal volume (~500 mL), IRV, ERV, residual volume.
- Vital capacity = TV + IRV + ERV; Total lung capacity = VC + RV.

## 4. Gas Transport
- **O₂:** ~97% via haemoglobin (oxyhaemoglobin); O₂ dissociation curve is sigmoid (Bohr effect — shifts right with high CO₂/H⁺/temp).
- **CO₂:** ~70% as bicarbonate, ~20–25% as carbamino-haemoglobin, ~7% dissolved.

## 5. Regulation & Disorders
- Respiratory centre in medulla. Disorders: asthma, emphysema, occupational (asbestosis, silicosis).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Gas exchange occurs at alveoli (not bronchi).
- Most CO₂ is transported as bicarbonate.
- Residual volume cannot be exhaled; not part of tidal volume.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Alveoli = exchange site.
- O₂ via Hb; CO₂ mostly as bicarbonate.
- VC = TV+IRV+ERV.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the respiratory volumes/capacities definitions and the bicarbonate fact for CO₂ transport.

notes.title: Body Fluids and Circulation — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Blood, the human heart, cardiac cycle and circulation.

## 1. Blood
- Plasma (~55%) + formed elements (RBC, WBC, platelets).
- RBC: biconcave, no nucleus (mammals), carry Hb. WBC: defence (granulocytes & agranulocytes). Platelets: clotting.
- Blood groups (ABO, Rh). Coagulation cascade (fibrinogen → fibrin).

## 2. Human Heart
- Four chambers (2 atria, 2 ventricles); myogenic heart. Valves prevent backflow (tricuspid, bicuspid, semilunar).
- **Conduction:** SA node (pacemaker) → AV node → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres.

## 3. Cardiac Cycle & ECG
- Systole (contraction), diastole (relaxation). Cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate (~5 L/min).
- ECG: P (atrial depolarisation), QRS (ventricular depolarisation), T (ventricular repolarisation).

## 4. Circulation
- **Double circulation:** pulmonary (heart–lungs) + systemic (heart–body).
- Arteries carry blood away from heart (oxygenated except pulmonary artery).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- SA node is the pacemaker.
- Pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood; pulmonary vein carries oxygenated.
- QRS complex = ventricular depolarisation (atrial repolarisation is masked).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Blood components & clotting.
- SA→AV→His→Purkinje.
- Double circulation; ECG waves.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Memorise the ECG waves and the cardiac conduction pathway — recurring NEET questions.

notes.title: Excretory Products and their Elimination — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Human excretory system and urine formation.

## 1. Modes of Excretion
- Ammonotelic (ammonia, aquatic), ureotelic (urea, mammals), uricotelic (uric acid, birds/reptiles — water conservation).

## 2. Human Excretory System
- Kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, urethra. Functional unit = **nephron** (~1 million per kidney).
- Nephron parts: glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, PCT, loop of Henle, DCT, collecting duct.

## 3. Urine Formation
- **Glomerular filtration** (GFR ~125 mL/min), **reabsorption** (PCT — most), **secretion** (H⁺, K⁺, NH₃).
- **Counter-current mechanism** (loop of Henle + vasa recta) concentrates urine.

## 4. Regulation
- ADH (water reabsorption), aldosterone (Na⁺ reabsorption), JGA & renin–angiotensin (blood pressure).

## 5. Disorders & Other Organs
- Uremia, kidney stones; dialysis. Other excretory organs: lungs (CO₂), skin, liver.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Functional unit of kidney is the nephron.
- Maximum reabsorption occurs in the PCT.
- ADH promotes water reabsorption (less, concentrated urine).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Ureotelic = humans.
- Filtration → reabsorption → secretion.
- ADH & aldosterone regulation.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Link each hormone (ADH, aldosterone, renin) to its specific action on the nephron.

notes.title: Neural Control and Coordination — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Nervous system: neurons, nerve impulse, and the human brain.

## 1. Neuron
- Structure: dendrites, cell body, axon. Types: myelinated & non-myelinated.
- **Resting potential** (−70 mV, more Na⁺ outside, K⁺ inside via Na⁺/K⁺ pump).

## 2. Nerve Impulse
- **Action potential:** depolarisation (Na⁺ influx) → repolarisation (K⁺ efflux). All-or-none.
- Conduction faster in myelinated axons (saltatory conduction at nodes of Ranvier).

## 3. Synapse
- **Electrical** (fast) and **chemical** (neurotransmitter like acetylcholine across synaptic cleft).

## 4. Central Nervous System
- **Brain:** forebrain (cerebrum — thinking; thalamus; hypothalamus — homeostasis), midbrain, hindbrain (cerebellum — balance; pons; medulla — vital centres).
- Spinal cord: reflex actions (reflex arc).

## 5. PNS & Reflex
- Somatic & autonomic (sympathetic 'fight or flight' vs parasympathetic 'rest').

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Resting potential maintained by Na⁺/K⁺ pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in).
- Saltatory conduction occurs in myelinated fibres (faster).
- Cerebellum controls balance; medulla controls heartbeat/respiration.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Action potential: Na⁺ in, K⁺ out.
- Synapse: chemical via neurotransmitter.
- Brain regions & functions.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Match brain parts to functions (cerebrum, cerebellum, hypothalamus, medulla) — frequent NEET matching MCQs.

notes.title: Chemical Coordination and Integration — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
The endocrine system and hormones.

## 1. Endocrine Glands (ductless)
- Hypothalamus, pituitary (master gland), pineal, thyroid, parathyroid, thymus, adrenal, pancreas, gonads.

## 2. Key Hormones
- **Pituitary:** GH (growth — excess: gigantism/acromegaly; deficiency: dwarfism), TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, prolactin, oxytocin, ADH.
- **Thyroid:** thyroxine (T₃/T₄) — needs iodine (deficiency → goitre).
- **Pancreas:** insulin (lowers blood glucose) & glucagon (raises). Insulin deficiency → diabetes mellitus.
- **Adrenal:** adrenaline/noradrenaline (emergency), cortisol, aldosterone.
- **Parathyroid:** PTH raises blood Ca²⁺.

## 3. Mechanism of Action
- Peptide hormones act via second messengers (cAMP) on membrane receptors; steroid hormones enter cells and act on genes.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Pituitary is the master gland, controlled by the hypothalamus.
- Insulin LOWERS blood glucose; glucagon raises it.
- Iodine deficiency causes goitre (thyroid).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Pituitary master gland.
- Insulin vs glucagon.
- Steroid vs peptide hormone action.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn each gland's hormone and the disorder from its excess/deficiency — high-yield for NEET.

notes.title: Locomotion and Movement — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Muscles, the skeletal system and joints.

## 1. Types of Movement & Muscle
- Amoeboid, ciliary, muscular.
- Muscle types: skeletal (striated, voluntary), smooth (involuntary), cardiac (striated, involuntary).

## 2. Muscle Contraction (Sliding Filament Theory)
- Actin (thin) slides over myosin (thick); sarcomere shortens.
- Requires Ca²⁺ (released from sarcoplasmic reticulum), ATP, and the troponin–tropomyosin system.

## 3. Skeletal System
- 206 bones in adult: axial (80: skull, vertebral column, ribs, sternum) + appendicular (126: limbs, girdles).

## 4. Joints
- Fibrous (immovable), cartilaginous (slightly movable), synovial (freely movable: ball-and-socket, hinge, pivot).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- During contraction, A-band length stays constant; I-band & H-zone shorten.
- Ca²⁺ triggers contraction by exposing actin binding sites.
- 206 bones: 80 axial + 126 appendicular.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Sliding filament theory; Ca²⁺ & ATP needed.
- 206 bones (axial + appendicular).
- Synovial joints freely movable.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember which bands change during contraction (A-band constant, I-band/H-zone shrink).

notes.title: Morphology of Flowering Plants — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
External structure of roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits and seeds.

## 1. Root, Stem, Leaf
- Root: tap (dicot) vs fibrous (monocot); modifications (storage, respiratory).
- Leaf: venation reticulate (dicot) vs parallel (monocot); phyllotaxy.

## 2. Inflorescence & Flower
- Racemose (main axis grows) vs cymose (terminates in a flower).
- Flower parts: calyx, corolla, androecium, gynoecium. Symmetry (actinomorphic/zygomorphic); aestivation.

## 3. Placentation & Fruit
- Types: marginal, axile, parietal, basal, free central.
- Fruit = ripened ovary; seed = ripened ovule.

## 4. Families (key for NEET)
- **Fabaceae** (pea), **Solanaceae** (potato/brinjal), **Liliaceae** — floral formulae & examples.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Tap root + reticulate venation = dicot; fibrous + parallel = monocot.
- Racemose has unlimited growth of the main axis.
- Learn floral formulae of the three families.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Dicot vs monocot features.
- Inflorescence types; placentation.
- Fabaceae, Solanaceae, Liliaceae.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
NEET regularly asks floral formulae/diagrams — memorise the three families thoroughly.

notes.title: Plant Growth and Development — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Growth phases and plant hormones (growth regulators).

## 1. Growth
- Phases: meristematic, elongation, maturation. Growth is measurable & quantifiable.
- Arithmetic vs geometric growth; growth rate (sigmoid curve).

## 2. Differentiation, Dedifferentiation, Redifferentiation
- Cells specialise; mature cells regain division ability (dedifferentiation) and re-specialise.

## 3. Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs)
- **Auxins** (apical dominance, rooting, parthenocarpy).
- **Gibberellins** (stem elongation, bolting, seed germination).
- **Cytokinins** (cell division, delay senescence).
- **Ethylene** (fruit ripening, abscission — gaseous hormone).
- **Abscisic acid (ABA)** (stress hormone, stomatal closure, dormancy — inhibitor).

## 4. Photoperiodism & Vernalisation
- Flowering response to day length; vernalisation = flowering promoted by cold treatment.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Auxin causes apical dominance; cytokinin overcomes it.
- Ethylene is a gaseous hormone (fruit ripening).
- ABA is the stress/inhibitory hormone (promotes dormancy & stomatal closure).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- 5 PGRs and their roles.
- Growth phases; sigmoid curve.
- Photoperiodism & vernalisation.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Link each hormone to its signature effect (auxin–apical dominance, ethylene–ripening, ABA–stress).
